| **[By Movement / School](movements.html) > [Modern](movements_modern.html) > Logical Positivism** |
| **Logical Positivism** (later also known as **Logical Empiricism**) is a **20th Century** school of philosophy that developed out of [Positivism](movements_positivism.html) and the early [Analytic Philosophy](movements_analytic.html) movement, and which campaigned for a systematic **reduction** of all human knowledge to **logical** and **scientific** foundations.  According to Logical Positivists, a statement is **meaningful** only if it is either purely **formal** (essentially, mathematics and logic) or capable of **empirical verification**. This effectively resulted in an almost complete **rejection** of [Metaphysics](branch_metaphysics.html) (and to a large extent [Ethics](branch_ethics.html)) on the grounds that it is **unverifiable**. Logical Positivism was also committed to the idea of **"Unified Science"**, or the development of a **common language** in which **all** scientific propositions can be expressed, usually by means of various **"reductions"** or **"explications"** of the terms of one science to the terms of another **more fundamental** one. For more details, see the section on the [doctrine of Logical Positivism](branch_logical_positivism.html).  The most important **early figures** in the development of Logical Positivism were the Bohemian-Austrian [Positivist](branch_positivism.html) philosopher **Ernst Mach** (1838 - 1916) and the Austrian [Ludwig Wittgenstein](philosophers_wittgenstein.html) (especially his ***"Tractatus"*** of 1921, a text of great importance for Logical Positivists).  The school grew from the discussions of the so-called **"Vienna Circle"** of **Moritz Schlick** (1882 - 1936) in the early 20th Century. A 1929 pamphlet jointly written by **Otto Neurath** (1882 - 1945), **Hans Hahn** (1979 - 1934) and **Rudolf Carnap** (1891 - 1970) brought together some of the **major proponents** of the movement and summarized the doctrines of the Vienna Circle at that time. The contemporaneous **Berlin Circle** of **Hans Reichenbach** (1891 - 1953) also propagated the new doctrines more widely in the 1920s and early 1930s.  [A. J. Ayer](philosophers_ayer.html) is considered responsible for the spread of Logical Positivism to **Britain**, and his 1936 book ***"Language, Truth and Logic"*** was very influential. Developments in **logic** and the foundations of **mathematics**, especially in the ***"Principia Mathematica"*** by the British philosophers [Bertrand Russell](philosophers_russell.html) and [Alfred North Whitehead](philosophers_whitehead.html), particularly impressed the more mathematically-minded Logical Positivists.  The movement **dispersed** in the late 1930's, mainly because of **political upheaval** and the untimely deaths of Hahn and Schlick. Logical Positivism was essential to the **development** of early [Analytic Philosophy](movements_analytic.html), with which it effectively merged. |
| **[Back to Top of Page](#Top)  [General](index.html) | [By Branch/Doctrine](branch.html) | [By Historical Period](historical.html) | [By Movement/School](movements.html) | [By Individual Philosopher](philosophers.html)**     **© 2008 Luke Mastin** |

**Logical Positivism** (later also known as **Logical Empiricism**) is a **20th Century** school of philosophy that developed out of [Positivism](movements_positivism.html) and the early [Analytic Philosophy](movements_analytic.html) movement, and which campaigned for a systematic **reduction** of all human knowledge to **logical** and **scientific** foundations.

According to Logical Positivists, a statement is **meaningful** only if it is either purely **formal** (essentially, mathematics and logic) or capable of **empirical verification**. This effectively resulted in an almost complete **rejection** of [Metaphysics](branch_metaphysics.html) (and to a large extent [Ethics](branch_ethics.html)) on the grounds that it is **unverifiable**. Logical Positivism was also committed to the idea of **"Unified Science"**, or the development of a **common language** in which **all** scientific propositions can be expressed, usually by means of various **"reductions"** or **"explications"** of the terms of one science to the terms of another **more fundamental** one. For more details, see the section on the [doctrine of Logical Positivism](branch_logical_positivism.html).

The most important **early figures** in the development of Logical Positivism were the Bohemian-Austrian [Positivist](branch_positivism.html) philosopher **Ernst Mach** (1838 - 1916) and the Austrian [Ludwig Wittgenstein](philosophers_wittgenstein.html) (especially his ***"Tractatus"*** of 1921, a text of great importance for Logical Positivists).

The school grew from the discussions of the so-called **"Vienna Circle"** of **Moritz Schlick** (1882 - 1936) in the early 20th Century. A 1929 pamphlet jointly written by **Otto Neurath** (1882 - 1945), **Hans Hahn** (1979 - 1934) and **Rudolf Carnap** (1891 - 1970) brought together some of the **major proponents** of the movement and summarized the doctrines of the Vienna Circle at that time. The contemporaneous **Berlin Circle** of **Hans Reichenbach** (1891 - 1953) also propagated the new doctrines more widely in the 1920s and early 1930s.

[A. J. Ayer](philosophers_ayer.html) is considered responsible for the spread of Logical Positivism to **Britain**, and his 1936 book ***"Language, Truth and Logic"*** was very influential. Developments in **logic** and the foundations of **mathematics**, especially in the ***"Principia Mathematica"*** by the British philosophers [Bertrand Russell](philosophers_russell.html) and [Alfred North Whitehead](philosophers_whitehead.html), particularly impressed the more mathematically-minded Logical Positivists.

The movement **dispersed** in the late 1930's, mainly because of **political upheaval** and the untimely deaths of Hahn and Schlick. Logical Positivism was essential to the **development** of early [Analytic Philosophy](movements_analytic.html), with which it effectively merged.

##### Philosophy & Science

# Einstein vs Logical Positivism

### **Rossen Vassilev Jr.** asks if modern physics has become too metaphysical.

Logical positivism was a philosophical movement of the 1920s and 30s which wanted to introduce the methodology of science and mathematics to philosophy. As part of this ambition, the Vienna Circle (*Wiener Kreis* in German) of logical positivists tried to purge philosophy of metaphysics – by which they meant any speculation that could not be tested using the methods of modern empirical science. The members of the Vienna Circle, including its nominal leader Moritz Schlick, found the speculative claims of traditional metaphysics, especially those based on religion, to be false, uncertain, or sterile. For Rudolph Carnap, another influential member of the Circle, “the (pseudo)statements of metaphysics do not serve for *the description of states of affairs*.” They are, like poetry and music, “in the domain of art and not in the domain of theory” (from ‘The End of Metaphysics?’ in *Western Philosophy: An Anthology*, edited by John Cottingham). Carnap confidently proclaimed that in the Circle’s new materialist philosophy of science, “a radical elimination of metaphysics is attained, which was not yet possible from the earlier anti-metaphysical standpoints.”

In fact, the logical positivists dismissed all non-scientific speculation altogether, not just in philosophy, insisting that all statements and theories are literally meaningless unless they can be logically verified or checked by experiment or observation. This is the so-called verification principle. A.J. Ayer was not a member of the Vienna Circle, but was powerfully influenced by it, and sprang its ideas upon the English-speaking world with his book *Language, Truth and Logic*. He argued that every verifiable proposition is meaningful (though it may be either true or false), and any unprovable claim, whether about science or metaphysics or the existence of God, is meaningless. Claims about ethics, he said, are also unverifiable so their only meaning can be as expressions of our emotional attitudes. According to verificationism, the meaning of any statement lies in its method of verification.

Soon, Karl Popper in his *Logic of Scientific Discovery* pointed out a problem with verification: no number of observations that agree with a theory can ever conclusively prove it true. A classic example is the claim that “all swans are white.” Not even a large number of sightings of white swans will prove this true, but even a single sighting of a non-white swan will disprove it (‘falsify it’). He argued that a “theory which is not refutable by any conceivable event is non-scientific. Irrefutability is not a virtue of a theory (as people often think) but a vice… the criterion of the scientific status of a theory is its falsifiability, or refutability, or testability.” So for Popper falsifiability, not verifiability, is the test which distinguishes genuine science from what Popper called ‘pseudo-science’ – or ‘metaphysics’.

In the second of four BBC interviews with Bryan Magee about logical positivism and its legacy, A.J. Ayer stressed that the Vienna Circle’s members “saw Einstein’s work on relativity and also the new quantum theory as a vindication of their approach.” It does seem that the replacement of Newtonian physics by Albert Einstein’s general theory of relativity had a revolutionary impact on logical positivism. As John Earman writes, “a brief examination of the actual history of logical positivism reveals that one of its most fundamental inspirations is precisely this Einsteinian revolution. The early writings of the logical positivists – of Schlick, Reichenbach, and Carnap, in particular – all focus on the theory of relativity, a theory whose revolutionary impact is explicitly recognized in the course of a polemic against their philosophical predecessors.” (*Inference, Explanation and Other Frustrations*, p.85, 1992)

But does the revolution in physics that Einstein began in 1905 *really* vindicate the principles of logical positivism? My contention is that it *contradicts* the Vienna Circle’s philosophy, particularly the Circle’s near fanatical commitment to the empiricist methodology of David Hume, Ernst Mach and Bertrand Russell, as well as Moritz Schlick’s principle of verifiability. Specifically, although many of the ground-breaking and highly exotic ideas coined by Einstein’s scientific successors – such as white holes, wormholes, dark matter, dark energy, subatomic strings, parallel universes, hidden dimensions of spacetime, and gravitational waves – may appear in the mathematical equations and calculations of theoretical physicists, in most cases no evidence has yet been found in the observable Universe to confirm their existence.

![logical positivism](/media/images/issues/133/logical positivism.jpg)  
Illustration © Jaime Raposo 2019. To see more of his art, please visit jaimeraposo.com

## Metaphysical Philosophy or Science Fiction?

According to renowned physicist Lee Smolin, co-founder of Canada’s Perimeter Institute for Theoretical Physics, this current move of physics away from Vienna Circle-style empiricism is due to “the triumph of a particular way of doing science that came to dominate physics in the 1940s. This style… favors virtuosity in calculating over reflection on hard conceptual problems” (*The Trouble with Physics*, 2006, pp.xxii). Mirroring the logical positivists’ devotion to empiricism, Smolin is highly skeptical of this ‘particular way of doing science’. It has led to what he calls ‘the current crisis’ in fundamental physics:

“New theories have been posited and explored, some in great detail, but none has been confirmed experimentally. And here is the crux of the problem: In science, for a theory to be believed, it must make a new prediction – different from those made by previous theories – for an experiment not yet done. For the experiment to be meaningful, we must be able to get an answer that disagrees with that prediction. When this is the case, we say that a theory is *falsifiable* – vulnerable to being shown false. The theory also has to be *confirmable*; it must be possible to verify a new prediction that only this theory makes. Only when a theory has been tested and the results agree with the theory do we advance the theory to the ranks of true theories”  
(*The Trouble with Physics* p.xiii)

In the light of this, many of the theories embraced by theoretical physicists today look like what Smolin calls ‘metaphysical fantasies’. One of the most prominent is the idea of multiple universes, also known as the ‘many-worlds hypothesis’ or ‘multiverse’ theory. Different versions of it have been promoted as an answer to puzzles in many branches of physics; it was the subject of an entire issue of *New Scientist* magazine in 2011, and among its many scientific supporters was the late Stephen Hawking, who worked on it in his last published paper. But in spite of all this, the multiverse hypothesis remains a purely speculative theory in the sense of not being checkable by direct observation. In fact it has no concrete empirical evidence to back it up. It cannot be proven using Schlick’s principle of verifiability; nor can it be falsified empirically in the way that Karl Popper believed any truly scientific theory should be. As a mere artifact of theoretical speculation and/or of mathematical models, it is more philosophy and metaphysics than testable empirical science. It borders on science fiction.

Another dubious theory gained popularity as a result of problems surrounding the Big Bang theory, concerning the relatively even distribution of matter observed throughout the visible Universe. This is something one would hardly expect to result from a gigantic explosion of energy concentrated into a point far smaller than an atom some 13.8 billion years ago. But our earliest radioastronomical ‘baby picture’ of how the Universe looked 380,000 years or so after the Big Bang, shows an even distribution of matter in every direction. To explain this anomaly, Alan Guth and other cosmologists proposed the so-called ‘inflation’ theory, according to which the newly created Universe initially expanded at a rate much faster even than the speed of light for a fraction of a second just after the Big Bang. But this *ad hoc* adjustment or ‘theoretical crutch’ (to use Thomas Kuhn’s apt phrase) fails to explain the even distribution of matter throughout the Universe, because the debris of a Big Bang-like explosion should still be rather unevenly and chaotically spread even if the Universe initially grew incredibly fast. Additionally, the new theory created other complications such as eleven dimensions of spacetime, countless Big Bangs and, again, the existence of numerous copies of each of us inhabiting multiple parallel universes. Smolin remarks that, “The theory of inflation made predictions that seemed dubious” (p.xi), and complained that this is hardly the stuff of which Popperian science is made:

“This is a sleight of hand by which they hope to convert an explanatory failure into an explanatory success. If we don’t understand… our universe, just presume our universe is a member of an infinite and unobservable ensemble of universes… There is so much wrong with this as a scientific hypothesis… it is hard to see how it could make any falsifiable predictions for doable experiments.”  
(quoted by John Horgan in *Scientific American*, January 4, 2015)

Moreover, consider what is perhaps the most popular theory among theoretical physicists today: string theory. According to Smolin, string theory “proposes that all the elementary particles arise from the vibrations of a single entity – a string” which is so infinitesimal that it is invisible even to the super-sophisticated instruments of modern science. But Smolin repudiates the scientific status of string theory because it “makes no new predictions that are testable by current – or even currently conceivable – experiments… Thus, no matter what the experiments show, string theory cannot be disproved. But the reverse also holds: no experiment will ever be able to prove it true” (*The Trouble with Physics*, p.xiv). Smolin also sees other equally fatal flaws in string theory. “Part of the reason string theory makes no new predictions is that it appears to come in an infinite number of versions… every single one disagrees with the present experimental data” (*Ibid*). He goes on to demystify the metaphysical claims of string theory despite its clearly dominant position in the world of physics today. “String theory… posits that the world is fundamentally different from the world we know. If string theory is right, the world has more dimensions and many more particles and forces than we have so far observed. Many string theorists talk and write as if the existence of these extra dimensions and particles were an assured fact, one that no good scientist can doubt. More than once, a string theorist has said to me something like ‘But do you mean you think it’s possible that there are not extra dimensions?’ In fact, neither theory nor experiment offers any evidence at all that extra dimensions exist.” (p.xvi)

Fundamental physics has been taken as the model for how all other sciences should develop. For dedicated empiricists like Smolin, it’s a tragedy that “despite our best efforts, what we know for certain about [fundamental physical] laws is no more than what we knew back in the 1970s” (p.viii). He blames this on the ‘arrogance’ of many of the string theorists. (p.269)

## It Is ‘All In The Math’?

During the total solar eclipse in the fall of 1919, British astrophysicists observed evidence that the Sun’s gravity bends the path of the light from other stars, confirming a key prediction of Einstein’s general theory of relativity. An insightful article by Richard Panek in the science magazine *Discover* in March 2008 described how Einstein interrupted a student who was trying to congratulate him for this scientific success, saying “But I knew the theory is correct.” But what, the student asked, if the observations had contradicted his calculations? Einstein replied, “Then I would have been sorry for the dear Lord ­­– the theory is correct.” It’s a smug rejection of the positivist principle of empirical verifiability. Einstein declared years later that “Our experience hitherto justifies us in believing that nature is the realization of the simplest conceivable mathematical ideas. I am convinced that we can discover by means of purely mathematical constructions the concepts and the laws connecting them with each other.” Panek nicely sums up the momentous implications of the leap from the more traditional method of empirical observation to Einstein’s faith in thought experiments and the ‘mathematical imagination’. “For thousands of years, researchers had studied the behavior of the heavens and sought to capture it in mathematical terms. Einstein reversed the challenge: he sought truth in equations and then trusted that studies of the heavens would back him up. Almost all of modern cosmology and theoretical physics follows from that leap of faith – or leap, perhaps, of reason.”

Smolin is not buying any such ‘leap of reason’ – unless it’s backed up by solid empirical evidence: by observations and experiments that prove or disprove any proposed hypothesis in strict compliance with both the Vienna Circle’s philosophy of science and Popper’s insights. He writes, “once you reason like this, you lose the ability to subject your theory to the kind of test that the history of science shows over and over again is required to winnow correct theories from beautiful but wrong ones. To do this, a theory must make specific and precise predictions that can either be confirmed or refuted. If there is a high risk of disconfirmation, then confirmation counts for a lot. If there is no risk of either, then there is no way to continue to do science” (*The Trouble with Physics*, p.169).

## Non-Positivist Conclusions

So is the theoretical physics embraced by today’s heirs to Einstein testable empirical science, metaphysical philosophy, or science fiction? Einstein’s heirs seem to have forgotten Einstein’s words of caution in 1933, that “Experience remains, of course, the sole criterion of the physical utility of a mathematical construction.” Lee Smolin adds his own warning: “Mathematics is our most useful tool, but the idea that it should be prophetic has done a lot of harm.” (*Scientific American*, January 4, 2015)

Panek’s *Discover* article concludes by identifying the enormous challenges faced by the troubled field of cosmology, which is now driven more by mathematics-based theory and ‘mathematical beauty’ than by hard-nosed observation-based empiricism. “These latest concepts all exist beautifully in the mathematics, but so far observers have identified no sign of them in the real world… How to move beyond slavish devotion to experience may have been Einstein’s greatest gift to the 20th century. How to bring mathematical imagination back down to earth may rate as his greatest challenge to the 21st.” If this challenge is not faced, then future achievements in fundamental physics – a field which is clearly “suffering from a surfeit of imagination and a deficit of data” (Horgan) – may move ever further from observation and testable prediction into ever more fanciful realms of metaphysical speculation. After all, what is one to think when America’s best-known living cosmologist, Dr Neil deGrasse Tyson, following the philosopher Nick Bostrom, says that the likelihood of the Universe being a digital simulation “may be very high… And if that’s the case, it is easy for me to imagine that everything in our lives is just the creation of some other entity for their entertainment.” Indeed, is this ‘simulation hypothesis’ itself a scientific theory, or pure science fiction?

© Rossen Vassilev Jr. 2019

*Rossen Vassilev is studying at Ohio University, in Athens, Ohio.*

--- SOURCE 4: Logical positivism | Philosophy of Science, Language & ... ---
URL: https://www.britannica.com/topic/logical-positivism

SUMMARY:
a philosophical movement that arose in Vienna in the 1920s and was characterized by the view that scientific knowledge is the only kind of factual knowledge.

FULL CONTENT:
![Encyclopedia Britannica](https://cdn.britannica.com/mendel/eb-logo/MendelNewThistleLogo.png)
![Encyclopedia Britannica](https://cdn.britannica.com/mendel/eb-logo/MendelNewThistleLogo.png)

# logical positivism

Our editors will review what you’ve submitted and determine whether to revise the article.

**logical positivism**, a philosophical movement that arose in Vienna in the 1920s and was characterized by the view that scientific knowledge is the only kind of factual knowledge and that all traditional [metaphysical](https://www.britannica.com/topic/metaphysics) doctrines are to be rejected as meaningless. A brief treatment of logical positivism follows. For full treatment, *see* [positivism: Logical positivism and logical empiricism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/positivism/Logical-positivism-and-logical-empiricism#ref68571).

Logical positivism differs from earlier forms of [empiricism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/empiricism) and [positivism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/positivism) (e.g., that of [David Hume](https://www.britannica.com/biography/David-Hume) and [Ernst Mach](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Ernst-Mach)) in holding that the ultimate basis of knowledge rests upon public experimental verification or confirmation rather than upon personal experience. It differs from the philosophies of [Auguste Comte](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Auguste-Comte) and [John Stuart Mill](https://www.britannica.com/biography/John-Stuart-Mill) in holding that [metaphysical](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/metaphysical) doctrines are not false but meaningless—that the “great unanswerable questions” about [substance](https://www.britannica.com/topic/substance-philosophy), [causality](https://www.britannica.com/topic/causation), [freedom](https://www.britannica.com/topic/free-will), and God are unanswerable just because they are not genuine questions at all. This last is a thesis about [language](https://www.britannica.com/topic/language), not about nature, and is based upon a general account of [meaning](https://www.britannica.com/topic/meaning) and of meaninglessness. All genuine [philosophy](https://www.britannica.com/topic/philosophy) (according to the group that came to be called the [Vienna Circle](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Vienna-Circle)) is a [critique](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/critique) of language, and (according to some of its leading members) its result is to show the unity of [science](https://www.britannica.com/science/science)—that all genuine knowledge about nature can be expressed in a single language common to all the sciences.

The [Vienna Circle](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Vienna-Circle), which produced its first [manifesto](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/manifesto) in 1929, had its origin in discussions among physicists and mathematicians before [World War I](https://www.britannica.com/event/World-War-I). The general conclusion was reached that the empiricism of Mill and Mach was inadequate, because it failed to explain mathematical and logical truths and because it did not account satisfactorily for the apparently [a priori](https://www.britannica.com/topic/a-priori-knowledge) element in natural science. In 1922 [Hans Hahn](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Hans-Hahn), one of the leaders of the Vienna Circle, laid before his students at the [University of Vienna](https://www.britannica.com/topic/University-of-Vienna) the *Logisch-philosophische Abhandlung* (1921; *[Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Tractatus-Logico-Philosophicus)*, 1922) of [Ludwig Wittgenstein](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Ludwig-Wittgenstein). This work introduced a new general theory of meaning—derived in part from the logical inquiries of [Giuseppe Peano](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Giuseppe-Peano), [Gottlob Frege](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Gottlob-Frege), [Bertrand Russell](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Bertrand-Russell), and [Alfred North Whitehead](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Alfred-North-Whitehead)—and gave the Vienna group its logical foundation. Most of the group’s members moved to the [United States](https://www.britannica.com/place/United-States) at the outset of [World War II](https://www.britannica.com/event/World-War-II). In the meantime, [disciples](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/disciples) had arisen in many other countries: in Poland, among the mathematical logicians; and in England, where [A.J. Ayer](https://www.britannica.com/biography/A-J-Ayer)’s *[Language, Truth, and Logic](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Language-Truth-and-Logic)* (1936) provided an excellent introduction to the views of the group. Interest in logical positivism began to wane in the 1950s, and by 1970 it had ceased to exist as a distinct philosophical movement.

![David Hume](https://cdn.britannica.com/08/131908-050-404073CE/David-Hume-oil-canvas-Allan-Ramsay-Scottish-1766.jpg)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 5: Logical positivism ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Logical_positivism

SUMMARY:
The movement emerged in the late 1920s among philosophers, scientists and mathematicians congregated within the Vienna Circle and Berlin Circle and flourished

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://en.wikipedia.org/static/images/donate/donate.gif)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)

# Logical positivism

**Logical positivism**, also known as **logical empiricism** or **neo-positivism**, was a philosophical movement, in the [empiricist](/wiki/Empiricism "Empiricism") tradition, that sought to formulate a [scientific philosophy](/wiki/Naturalized_epistemology "Naturalized epistemology") in which philosophical discourse would be, in the perception of its proponents, as authoritative and meaningful as [empirical science](/wiki/Empirical_science "Empirical science").[[1]](#cite_note-Friedman-pxiv-1)

Logical positivism's central thesis was the [verification principle](/wiki/Verificationism "Verificationism"), also known as the "verifiability criterion of meaning", according to which a statement is *cognitively meaningful* only if it can be verified through [empirical observation](/wiki/Observation "Observation") or if it is a [tautology](/wiki/Tautology_(logic) "Tautology (logic)") (true by virtue of its own [meaning](/wiki/Semantics "Semantics") or its own [logical form](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax")).[[2]](#cite_note-2) The verifiability criterion thus rejected statements of [metaphysics](/wiki/Metaphysics "Metaphysics"), [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology"), [ethics](/wiki/Ethics "Ethics") and [aesthetics](/wiki/Aesthetics "Aesthetics") as *cognitively meaningless* in terms of [truth value](/wiki/Truth_value "Truth value") or [factual](/wiki/Fact "Fact") content. Despite its ambition to overhaul philosophy by mimicking the structure and process of empirical science, logical positivism became erroneously stereotyped as an agenda to regulate the scientific process and to place strict standards on it.[[1]](#cite_note-Friedman-pxiv-1)

The movement emerged in the late 1920s among [philosophers](/wiki/Philosopher "Philosopher"), [scientists](/wiki/Scientist "Scientist") and [mathematicians](/wiki/Mathematician "Mathematician") congregated within the [Vienna Circle](/wiki/Vienna_Circle "Vienna Circle") and [Berlin Circle](/wiki/Berlin_Circle_(philosophy) "Berlin Circle (philosophy)") and flourished in several European centres through the 1930s. By the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), many of its members had settled in the [English-speaking world](/wiki/Anglosphere "Anglosphere") and the project shifted to less radical goals within the [philosophy of science](/wiki/Philosophy_of_science "Philosophy of science").[[3]](#cite_note-sep-vienna-circle-3)

By the 1950s, problems identified within logical positivism's central tenets became seen as intractable, drawing escalating criticism among leading philosophers, notably from [Willard van Orman Quine](/wiki/Willard_van_Orman_Quine "Willard van Orman Quine") and [Karl Popper](/wiki/Karl_Popper "Karl Popper"), and even from within the movement, from [Carl Hempel](/wiki/Carl_Hempel "Carl Hempel"). These problems would remain unresolved, precipitating the movement's eventual decline and abandonment by the 1960s. In 1967, philosopher [John Passmore](/wiki/John_Passmore "John Passmore") pronounced logical positivism "dead, or as dead as a philosophical movement ever becomes".[[4]](#cite_note-hanfling-4)

## Contents

## Origins

Logical positivism emerged in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") and [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria") amid a cultural background characterised by the dominance of [Hegelian metaphysics](/wiki/Hegelian_metaphysics "Hegelian metaphysics") and the work of Hegelian successors such as [F. H. Bradley](/wiki/F._H._Bradley "F. H. Bradley"), whose [metaphysics](/wiki/Metaphysics "Metaphysics") portrayed the world without reference to [empirical observation](/wiki/Observation "Observation").[[5]](#cite_note-Suppe2-5) The late 19th century also saw the emergence of [neo-Kantianism](/wiki/Neo-Kantianism "Neo-Kantianism") as a philosophical movement, in the [rationalist](/wiki/Rationalism "Rationalism") tradition.[[6]](#cite_note-uebel3-6)

The logical positivist program established its theoretical foundations in the [empiricism](/wiki/Empiricism "Empiricism") of [David Hume](/wiki/David_Hume "David Hume"), [Auguste Comte](/wiki/Auguste_Comte "Auguste Comte") and [Ernst Mach](/wiki/Ernst_Mach "Ernst Mach"), along with the [positivism](/wiki/Positivism "Positivism") of Comte and Mach, defining its exemplar of science in [Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein")'s [general theory of relativity](/wiki/General_theory_of_relativity "General theory of relativity").[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[6]](#cite_note-uebel3-6) In accordance with Mach's [phenomenalism](/wiki/Phenomenalism "Phenomenalism"), whereby [material objects](/wiki/Material_object "Material object") exist only as [sensory stimuli](/wiki/Sense_data "Sense data") rather than as observable entities in the [real world](/wiki/Reality "Reality"), logical positivists took all scientific knowledge to be only sensory experience.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Further influence came from [Percy Bridgman](/wiki/Percy_Bridgman "Percy Bridgman")'s [operationalism](/wiki/Operationalism "Operationalism")—whereby a concept is not knowable unless it can be measured experimentally—as well as [Immanuel Kant](/wiki/Immanuel_Kant "Immanuel Kant")'s perspectives on [aprioricity](/wiki/A_priori "A priori").[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[6]](#cite_note-uebel3-6)

[Ludwig Wittgenstein](/wiki/Ludwig_Wittgenstein "Ludwig Wittgenstein")'s *[Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus](/wiki/Tractatus_Logico-Philosophicus "Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus")* established the theoretical foundations for the [verifiability principle](/wiki/Verifiability_principle "Verifiability principle").[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) His work introduced the view of philosophy as "critique of language", discussing theoretical distinctions between intelligible and nonsensical discourse. *Tractatus* adhered to a [correspondence theory of truth](/wiki/Correspondence_theory_of_truth "Correspondence theory of truth"), as opposed to a [coherence theory of truth](/wiki/Coherence_theory_of_truth "Coherence theory of truth"). Logical positivists were also influenced by Wittgenstein's interpretation of [probability](/wiki/Probability "Probability") though, according to [Neurath](/wiki/Otto_Neurath "Otto Neurath"), some objected to the metaphysics in *Tractatus*.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## History

### Vienna and Berlin Circles

The [Vienna Circle](/wiki/Vienna_Circle "Vienna Circle") was led principally by [Moritz Schlick](/wiki/Moritz_Schlick "Moritz Schlick"), congregating around the [University of Vienna](/wiki/University_of_Vienna "University of Vienna") and at the [Café Central](/wiki/Caf%C3%A9_Central "Café Central"). A manifesto written by [Otto Neurath](/wiki/Otto_Neurath "Otto Neurath"), [Hans Hahn](/wiki/Hans_Hahn_(mathematician) "Hans Hahn (mathematician)") and [Rudolf Carnap](/wiki/Rudolf_Carnap "Rudolf Carnap") in 1929 summarised the Vienna Circle's positions. Schlick had originally held a [neo-Kantian](/wiki/Neo-Kantianism "Neo-Kantianism") position, but later converted, via Carnap's 1928 book *Der logische Aufbau der Welt* (*The Logical Structure of the World*). The Viennese maintained closely cooperative ties with the [Berlin Circle](/wiki/Berlin_Circle "Berlin Circle"), among whom [Hans Reichenbach](/wiki/Hans_Reichenbach "Hans Reichenbach") was pre-eminent. [Carl Hempel](/wiki/Carl_Hempel "Carl Hempel"), who studied under Reichenbach in Germany, was also to prove influential in the movement's later history.[[13]](#cite_note-sep-hempel-13) A friendly but tenacious critic of the movement was [Karl Popper](/wiki/Karl_Popper "Karl Popper"), whom Neurath nicknamed the "Official Opposition".[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Early in the movement, Carnap, Hahn, Neurath and others recognised that the [verifiability criterion](/wiki/Logical_positivist#Verifiability_Criterion_of_Meaning "Logical positivist") was too stringent in that it rejected [universal statements](/wiki/Universal_generalization "Universal generalization"), which are vital to [scientific hypothesis](/wiki/Hypothesis "Hypothesis").[[15]](#cite_note-Sarkar2005-15) A radical *left wing* emerged from the Vienna Circle, led by Neurath and Carnap, who proposed revisions to weaken the criterion, a program they referred to as the "liberalisation of empiricism". A conservative *right wing*, led by Schlick and [Waismann](/wiki/Friedrich_Waismann "Friedrich Waismann"), instead sought to classify universal statements as analytic truths, thereby to reconcile them with the existing criterion.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Within the liberal wing Carnap emphasised [fallibilism](/wiki/Fallibilism "Fallibilism"), as well as [pragmatics](/wiki/Pragmatics "Pragmatics"), which he considered integral to [empiricism](/wiki/Empiricism "Empiricism"). Neurath prescribed a move from [Mach](/wiki/Ernst_Mach "Ernst Mach")'s [phenomenalism](/wiki/Phenomenalism "Phenomenalism") to [physicalism](/wiki/Physicalism "Physicalism"), though this would be opposed by Schlick. As Neurath and Carnap sought to pose science toward social reform, the split in the Vienna Circle also reflected political differences.[[15]](#cite_note-Sarkar2005-15)

Both Schlick and Carnap had been influenced by and sought to define logical positivism versus the neo-Kantianism of [Ernst Cassirer](/wiki/Ernst_Cassirer "Ernst Cassirer"), the contemporary leading figure of the [Marburg school](/wiki/Marburg_school "Marburg school"), and against [Edmund Husserl](/wiki/Edmund_Husserl "Edmund Husserl")'s [phenomenology](/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy) "Phenomenology (philosophy)"). Logical positivists especially opposed [Martin Heidegger](/wiki/Martin_Heidegger "Martin Heidegger")'s obscure metaphysics, the epitome of what they had rejected through their epistemological doctrines. In the early 1930s, Carnap debated Heidegger over "metaphysical pseudosentences".[[17]](#cite_note-Friedman-pxii-17)

### Anglosphere

As the movement's first emissary to the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World"), Moritz Schlick visited [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University") in 1929, yet otherwise remained in Vienna and was murdered in 1936 at [the University](/wiki/University_of_Vienna "University of Vienna") by a former student, [Johann Nelböck](/wiki/Johann_Nelb%C3%B6ck "Johann Nelböck"), who was reportedly deranged.[[17]](#cite_note-Friedman-pxii-17) That year, [A. J. Ayer](/wiki/A._J._Ayer "A. J. Ayer"), a British attendee at various Vienna Circle meetings since 1933, published *[Language, Truth and Logic](/wiki/Language,_Truth_and_Logic "Language, Truth and Logic")*, which imported logical positivism to the [English-speaking world](/wiki/English-speaking_world "English-speaking world"). In 1933, the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party")'s rise to power in Germany had triggered flight of intellectuals, which accelerated upon Germany's [annexation of Austria](/wiki/Annexation_of_Austria "Annexation of Austria") in 1938.[[17]](#cite_note-Friedman-pxii-17) The logical positivists, many of whom were [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish "Jewish"), were targeted and continued flight throughout the pre-war period. Their philosophy thus became dominant in the [English-speaking world](/wiki/English-speaking_world "English-speaking world").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

By the late 1930s, many in the movement had replaced [phenomenalism](/wiki/Phenomenalism "Phenomenalism") with Neurath's [physicalism](/wiki/Physicalism "Physicalism"), whereby [material objects](/wiki/Material_object "Material object") are not reducible to [sensory stimuli](/wiki/Stimulus_(physiology) "Stimulus (physiology)") but exist as publicly observable entities in the [real world](/wiki/Reality "Reality"). Neurath settled in England, where he died in 1945. Carnap, Reichenbach and Hempel settled permanently in America.[[17]](#cite_note-Friedman-pxii-17)

### Post-war period

Following the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_2 "World War 2"), logical positivism—now referred to by some as *logical empiricism*—turned to less radical objectives in the [philosophy of science](/wiki/Philosophy_of_science "Philosophy of science"). Led by [Carl Hempel](/wiki/Carl_Gustav_Hempel "Carl Gustav Hempel"), who expounded the [covering law model](/wiki/Covering_law_model "Covering law model") of [scientific explanation](/wiki/Scientific_explanation "Scientific explanation"), the movement became a major underpinning of [analytic philosophy](/wiki/Analytic_philosophy "Analytic philosophy") in the English-speaking world[[19]](#cite_note-19) and its influence extended beyond philosophy into the [social sciences](/wiki/Social_science "Social science"). At the same time, the movement drew intensifying scrutiny over its central problems[[20]](#cite_note-Smith1986-20)[[21]](#cite_note-Bunge1996-21) and its doctrines were increasingly criticised, most trenchantly by [Willard Van Orman Quine](/wiki/Willard_Van_Orman_Quine "Willard Van Orman Quine"), [Norwood Hanson](/wiki/Norwood_Russell_Hanson "Norwood Russell Hanson"), [Karl Popper](/wiki/Karl_Raimund_Popper "Karl Raimund Popper"), [Thomas Kuhn](/wiki/Thomas_Samuel_Kuhn "Thomas Samuel Kuhn") and [Carl Hempel](/wiki/Carl_Gustav_Hempel "Carl Gustav Hempel").[[3]](#cite_note-sep-vienna-circle-3)

## Principles

### Verification and Confirmation

#### Verifiability Criterion of Meaning

According to the [verifiability criterion of meaning](/wiki/Verificationism "Verificationism"), a statement is *cognitively meaningful* only if it is either verifiable by [empirical observation](/wiki/Observation "Observation") or is an [analytic truth](/wiki/Analytic_proposition "Analytic proposition") (i.e. true by virtue of its own [meaning](/wiki/Semantics "Semantics") or its own [logical form](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax")).[[22]](#cite_note-22) *[Cognitive meaningfulness](/wiki/Meaning_(philosophy_of_language) "Meaning (philosophy of language)")* was defined variably: possessing [truth value](/wiki/Truth_value "Truth value"); or corresponding to a possible state of affairs; or intelligible or understandable as are scientific statements. Other types of meaning—for instance, emotive, expressive or figurative—were dismissed from further review.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

[Metaphysics](/wiki/Metaphysics "Metaphysics"), [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology"), as well as much of [ethics](/wiki/Ethics "Ethics") and [aesthetics](/wiki/Aesthetics "Aesthetics") failed this criterion, and so were found cognitively meaningless and only *emotively meaningful* (though, notably, Schlick considered ethical and aesthetic statements cognitively meaningful).[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) Ethics and aesthetics were considered subjective preferences, while theology and metaphysics contained "pseudostatements" that were neither true nor false. Thus, logical positivism indirectly asserted [Hume's law](/wiki/Hume%27s_law "Hume's law"), the principle that [factual](/wiki/Fact "Fact") statements cannot justify [evaluative](/wiki/Axiology "Axiology") statements, and that the two are separated by an unbridgeable gap. [A. J. Ayer](/wiki/A._J._Ayer "A. J. Ayer")'s *[Language, Truth and Logic](/wiki/Language,_Truth_and_Logic "Language, Truth and Logic")* (1936) presented an extreme version of this principle—the [boo/hooray doctrine](/wiki/Emotivism "Emotivism")—whereby all evaluative judgments are merely emotional reactions.[[26]](#cite_note-Ayer1-26)

#### Revisions to the criterion

Logical positivists in the Vienna Circle recognised quickly that the verifiability criterion was too restrictive.[[13]](#cite_note-sep-hempel-13) Specifically, [universal statements](/wiki/Universal_generalization "Universal generalization") were noted to be empirically unverifiable, rendering vital domains of science and [reason](/wiki/Reason "Reason"), such as [scientific hypothesis](/wiki/Hypothesis "Hypothesis"), *cognitively meaningless* under verificationism. This would pose significant problems for the logical positivist program, absent revisions to its criterion of meaning.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In his 1936 and 1937 papers, *Testability and Meaning*, [Carnap](/wiki/Rudolf_Carnap "Rudolf Carnap") proposed *confirmation* in place of verification, determining that, though universal laws cannot be verified, they can be confirmed.[[15]](#cite_note-Sarkar2005-15) Carnap employed abundant logical and mathematical tools to research an [inductive logic](/wiki/Inductive_reasoning "Inductive reasoning") that would account for probability according to *degrees of confirmation*. However, he was never able to formulate a model. In Carnap's inductive logic, a universal law's degree of confirmation was always zero.[[28]](#cite_note-IEP-Carnap-28) The formulation of what eventually came to be called the "criterion of cognitive significance", stemming from this research, took three decades (Hempel 1950, Carnap 1956, Carnap 1961).[[15]](#cite_note-Sarkar2005-15) [Carl Hempel](/wiki/Carl_Hempel "Carl Hempel"), who became a prominent critic of the logical positivist movement, elucidated the [paradox of confirmation](/wiki/Paradox_of_the_ravens "Paradox of the ravens").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In his 1936 book, *Language, Truth and Logic*, [A. J. Ayer](/wiki/A._J._Ayer "A. J. Ayer") distinguished *strong* and *weak* verification. He stipulated that, "A proposition is said to be verifiable, in the strong sense of the term, if, and only if, its truth could be conclusively established by experience", but is verifiable in the weak sense "if it is possible for experience to render it probable". He would add that, "no proposition, other than a [tautology](/wiki/Tautology_(logic) "Tautology (logic)"), can possibly be anything more than a probable [hypothesis](/wiki/Hypothesis "Hypothesis")". Thus, he would conclude that all are open to weak verification.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Analytic-synthetic distinction

In [theories of justification](/wiki/Justification_(epistemology) "Justification (epistemology)"), *[a priori](/wiki/A_priori_knowledge "A priori knowledge")* statements are those that can be known independently of [observation](/wiki/Observation "Observation"), contrasting with *[a posteriori](/wiki/A_posteriori "A posteriori")* statements, which are dependent on observation. Statements may also be categorised into *[analytic](/wiki/Analytic-synthetic_distinction "Analytic-synthetic distinction")* and *[synthetic](/wiki/Analytic-synthetic_distinction "Analytic-synthetic distinction")*: Analytic statements are true by virtue of their own [meaning](/wiki/Semantics "Semantics") or their own [logical form](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax"), therefore are [tautologies](/wiki/Tautology_(logic) "Tautology (logic)") that are true by [necessity](/wiki/Logical_truth "Logical truth") but uninformative about the world. Synthetic statements, in comparison, are [contingent](/wiki/Contingency_(philosophy) "Contingency (philosophy)") propositions that refer to a state of facts concerning the world.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

[David Hume](/wiki/David_Hume "David Hume") proposed an unambiguous distinction between analytic and synthetic, categorising knowledge exclusively as either "relations of ideas" (which are *a priori*, analytic and [abstract](/wiki/Abstract_and_concrete "Abstract and concrete")) or "matters of fact and real existence" (*a posteriori*, synthetic and [concrete](/wiki/Abstract_and_concrete "Abstract and concrete")), a classification referred to as [Hume's fork](/wiki/Hume%27s_fork "Hume's fork").[[33]](#cite_note-Flew-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) [Immanuel Kant](/wiki/Immanuel_Kant "Immanuel Kant") identified a further category of knowledge: [Synthetic *a priori*](/wiki/Synthetic_a_priori "Synthetic a priori") statements, which are informative about the world, but known without observation. This principle is encapsulated in Kant's [transcendental idealism](/wiki/Transcendental_idealism "Transcendental idealism"), which attributes the mind a constructive role in [phenomena](/wiki/Phenomena "Phenomena") whereby [intuitive](/wiki/Rationalism#Intuition-deduction_thesis "Rationalism") truths—including synthetic *a priori* conceptions of [space](/wiki/Space "Space") and [time](/wiki/Time "Time")—function as an interpretative filter for an observer's experience of the world.[[35]](#cite_note-35) His thesis would serve to rescue [Newton's law of universal gravitation](/wiki/Newton%27s_law_of_universal_gravitation "Newton's law of universal gravitation") from Hume's [problem of induction](/wiki/Problem_of_induction "Problem of induction") by determining [uniformity of nature](/wiki/Uniformitarianism "Uniformitarianism") to be in the category of *a priori* knowledge.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The Vienna Circle rejected Kant's conception of synthetic *a priori* knowledge given its incompatibility with the [verifiability criterion](/wiki/Verificationism "Verificationism").[[37]](#cite_note-37) Yet, they adopted the Kantian position of defining mathematics and logic—ordinarily considered synthetic truths—as *a priori*.[[38]](#cite_note-38) [Carnap](/wiki/Rudolf_Carnap "Rudolf Carnap")'s solution to this discrepancy would be to reinterpret logical truths as tautologies, redefining logic as analytic, building upon theoretical foundations established in [Wittgenstein](/wiki/Ludwig_Wittgenstein "Ludwig Wittgenstein")'s *Tractatus*. Mathematics, in turn, would be reduced to logic through the [logicist approach](/wiki/Logical_Positivism#Logicism "Logical Positivism") proposed by [Gottlob Frege](/wiki/Gottlob_Frege "Gottlob Frege"). In effect, Carnap's reconstruction of analyticity expounded Hume's fork, affirming its analytic-synthetic distinction. This would be critically important in rendering the verification principle compatible with mathematics and logic.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### Observation-theory distinction

[Carnap](/wiki/Rudolf_Carnap "Rudolf Carnap") devoted much of his career to the cornerstone [doctrine](/wiki/Doctrine "Doctrine") of *rational reconstruction*, whereby scientific theories can be formalised into [predicate logic](/wiki/Predicate_logic "Predicate logic") and the components of a theory categorised into *[observation terms](/wiki/Ramsey_sentence "Ramsey sentence")* and *[theoretical terms](/wiki/Ramsey_sentence "Ramsey sentence")*.[[40]](#cite_note-sep-recon-40) Observation terms are specified by direct observation and thus assumed to have fixed empirical definitions, whereas theoretical terms refer to the [unobservables](/wiki/Unobservable "Unobservable") of a theory, including [abstract](/wiki/Abstract_and_concrete "Abstract and concrete") conceptions such as [mathematical formulas](/wiki/Formula "Formula"). The two categories of [primitive terms](/wiki/Primitive_notion "Primitive notion") would be interconnected in meaning via a [deductive](/wiki/Deductive_reasoning "Deductive reasoning") interpretative framework, referred to as *correspondence rules*.[[41]](#cite_note-carnap-win-41)

Early in his research, Carnap postulated that correspondence rules could be used to define theoretical terms from observation terms, contending that scientific knowledge could be unified by [reducing](/wiki/Reductionism "Reductionism") theoretical laws to "protocol sentences" grounded in observable facts. He would soon abandon this model of reconstruction, suggesting instead that theoretical terms could be defined implicitly by the [axioms](/wiki/Axioms "Axioms") of a theory. Furthermore, that observation terms could, in some cases, garner meaning from theoretical terms via correspondence rules.[[42]](#cite_note-carnap-lt-42) Here, definition is said to be 'implicit' in that the axioms serve to exclude those interpretations that falsify the theory. Thus, axioms define theoretical terms indirectly by restricting the set of possible interpretations to those that are true interpretations.[[41]](#cite_note-carnap-win-41)

By reconstructing the [semantics](/wiki/Semantics "Semantics") of scientific language, Carnap's thesis builds upon earlier research in the reconstruction of [syntax](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax"), referring to [Bertrand Russell](/wiki/Bertrand_Russell "Bertrand Russell")'s [logical atomism](/wiki/Logical_atomism "Logical atomism")—the view that statements in [natural language](/wiki/Natural_language "Natural language") can be converted to standardised subunits of meaning assembled via a [logical syntax](/wiki/Logical_syntax "Logical syntax").[[43]](#cite_note-43) Rational reconstruction is sometimes referred to as the *[received view](/wiki/Received_view "Received view")* or *syntactic view of theories* in the context of subsequent work by [Carl Hempel](/wiki/Carl_Hempel "Carl Hempel"), [Ernest Nagel](/wiki/Ernest_Nagel "Ernest Nagel") and [Herbert Feigl](/wiki/Herbert_Feigl "Herbert Feigl").[[40]](#cite_note-sep-recon-40)

### Logicism

By [reducing](/wiki/Reductionism "Reductionism") mathematics to logic, [Bertrand Russell](/wiki/Bertrand_Russell "Bertrand Russell") sought to convert the mathematical formulas of [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics") to [symbolic logic](/wiki/Symbolic_logic "Symbolic logic"). [Gottlob Frege](/wiki/Gottlob_Frege "Gottlob Frege") began this program of [logicism](/wiki/Logicism "Logicism"), continuing it with Russell, but eventually lost interest. Russell then continued it with [Alfred North Whitehead](/wiki/Alfred_North_Whitehead "Alfred North Whitehead") in their *[Principia Mathematica](/wiki/Principia_Mathematica "Principia Mathematica")*, inspiring some of the more mathematical logical positivists, such as [Hans Hahn](/wiki/Hans_Hahn_(mathematician) "Hans Hahn (mathematician)") and [Rudolf Carnap](/wiki/Rudolf_Carnap "Rudolf Carnap").[[44]](#cite_note-Hintikka-44)

Carnap's early anti-metaphysical works employed Russell's [theory of types](/wiki/Type_theory "Type theory").[[45]](#cite_note-45) Like Russell, Carnap envisioned a universal language that could reconstruct mathematics and thereby encode physics. Yet [Kurt Gödel](/wiki/Kurt_G%C3%B6del "Kurt Gödel")'s [incompleteness theorem](/wiki/Incompleteness_theorem "Incompleteness theorem") showed this to be impossible, except in trivial cases, and [Alfred Tarski](/wiki/Alfred_Tarski "Alfred Tarski")'s [undefinability theorem](/wiki/Undefinability_theorem "Undefinability theorem") finally undermined all hopes of reducing mathematics to logic. Thus, a universal language failed to stem from Carnap's 1934 work *Logische Syntax der Sprache* (*Logical Syntax of Language*). Still, some logical positivists, including [Carl Hempel](/wiki/Carl_Hempel "Carl Hempel"), continued support of logicism.[[44]](#cite_note-Hintikka-44)

## Philosophy of science

The logical positivist movement shed much of its revolutionary zeal following the defeat of Nazism and the decline of rival philosophies that sought radical reform, notably [Marburg](/wiki/Marburg_school "Marburg school") [neo-Kantianism](/wiki/Neo-Kantianism "Neo-Kantianism"), [Husserlian](/wiki/Edmund_Husserl "Edmund Husserl") [phenomenology](/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy) "Phenomenology (philosophy)") and [Heidegger](/wiki/Martin_Heidegger "Martin Heidegger")'s [existential](/wiki/Existentialism "Existentialism") [hermeneutics](/wiki/Hermeneutics "Hermeneutics"). Hosted in the climate of American [pragmatism](/wiki/Pragmatism "Pragmatism") and [common sense](/wiki/Common_sense "Common sense") [empiricism](/wiki/Empiricism "Empiricism"), its proponents no longer crusaded to revise traditional philosophy into a radical *scientific philosophy*, but became respectable members of a new philosophical subdiscipline, *[philosophy of science](/wiki/Philosophy_of_science "Philosophy of science")*.[[1]](#cite_note-Friedman-pxiv-1) Receiving support from [Ernest Nagel](/wiki/Ernest_Nagel "Ernest Nagel"), they were especially influential in the [social sciences](/wiki/Social_sciences "Social sciences").[[46]](#cite_note-Novick-p546-46)

### Scientific explanation

[Carl Hempel](/wiki/Carl_Hempel "Carl Hempel") was prominent in the development of the [deductive-nomological](/wiki/Deductive-nomological_model "Deductive-nomological model") (DN) model, then the foremost model of [scientific explanation](/wiki/Models_of_scientific_inquiry "Models of scientific inquiry") defended even among critics of neo-positivism such as [Popper](/wiki/Karl_Popper "Karl Popper").[[47]](#cite_note-SEPWoodward-47) According to the DN model, a scientific explanation is valid only if it takes the form of a [deductive inference](/wiki/Deductive_reasoning "Deductive reasoning") from a set of explanatory [premises](/wiki/Premise "Premise") (*explanans*) to the observation or theory to be explained (*explanandum*).[[48]](#cite_note-Suppe-48) The model stipulates that the premises must refer to at least one [law](/wiki/Scientific_law "Scientific law"), which it defines as an [unrestricted generalization](/wiki/Enumerative_induction "Enumerative induction") of the [conditional](/wiki/Conditional_sentence "Conditional sentence") form: "If *A*, then *B*".[[49]](#cite_note-49) Laws therefore differ from mere *regularities* ("George always carries only $1 bills in his wallet") which do not necessarily support [counterfactual](/wiki/Counterfactual "Counterfactual") claims.[[50]](#cite_note-50) Furthermore, laws must be empirically verifiable in compliance with the verification principle.[[48]](#cite_note-Suppe-48)

The DN model ignores causal mechanisms beyond the principle of [constant conjunction](/wiki/Constant_conjunction "Constant conjunction") ("first event *A* and then always event *B*") in accordance with the [Humean](/wiki/David_Hume "David Hume") [empiricist](/wiki/Empiricism "Empiricism") postulate that, though sequences of events are observable, the underpinning [causal principles](/wiki/Causality "Causality") are not.[[47]](#cite_note-SEPWoodward-47) Hempel stated that well-formulated natural laws (empirically confirmed regularities) are sat

enriched_points.expanded_content: Logical Positivism emerged in the 20th century, advocating that meaningful statements must be empirically verifiable or analytically true, effectively dismissing metaphysical claims as meaningless.
Karl Popper introduced the concept of falsifiability, asserting that scientific theories must be testable and capable of being proven false, differentiating science from non-science.
Ethics gained prominence in scientific discourse, emphasizing the moral responsibilities of researchers towards human subjects, society, and the environment, leading to stricter ethical guidelines.
The 20th century saw an emphasis on interdisciplinary approaches, combining insights from different fields like physics, biology, and philosophy to enhance understanding and innovation in science.
